hero.tagline: Maryland's small claims court offers a simplified process for claims up to $5,000, with informal procedures and expedited timelines.

at_a_glance.label: Most you can sue for
at_a_glance.detail: Same cap for individuals and businesses

at_a_glance.label: Appeal window
at_a_glance.detail: Trial de novo in circuit court

at_a_glance.label: Recent change
at_a_glance.detail: No major changes in the last 3 years

limits.split_claims_explanation: If your claim exceeds $5,000, you cannot split it into multiple small claims cases. Instead, you must file in the District Court's regular civil docket or the Circuit Court, depending on the amount.

claim_categories.title: Contracts and money owed
claim_categories.blurb: Maryland allows you 3 years to file claims related to contracts and debts. This includes written and oral agreements, personal loans, and unpaid invoices.

claims.name: Breach of a written contract
claims.example: A contractor signed a $4,000 contract to remodel your kitchen, took the deposit, and never finished.
claims.notes: You have 3 years from the date of breach to file. Bring the signed contract, payment proof, and any correspondence showing the breach.

claims.name: Breach of an oral contract
claims.example: You agreed verbally to lend a friend $2,000, which they promised to repay in six months, but they haven't.
claims.notes: You have 3 years from the date of breach to file. Gather any evidence of the agreement, such as emails or witness statements.

claims.name: Unpaid personal loan
claims.example: You loaned a colleague $3,500 with the understanding they'd repay in a year, but they haven't.
claims.notes: You have 3 years from the date repayment was due to file. Provide any written agreement or proof of the loan.

claims.name: Default on a promissory note
claims.example: A borrower signed a promissory note to repay $4,500 over two years but stopped making payments after six months.
claims.notes: You have 3 years from the date of default to file. Present the original promissory note and records of payments made.

claims.name: Unpaid invoice for services rendered
claims.example: You provided graphic design services worth $2,800 to a client who hasn't paid the invoice issued three months ago.
claims.notes: You have 3 years from the date the payment was due to file. Bring the invoice, service agreement, and any communication regarding payment.

claims.name: Unpaid balance on an open account
claims.example: A customer has an outstanding balance of $4,200 on their account with your business and has ignored payment requests.
claims.notes: You have 3 years from the date of the last payment or charge to file. Provide account statements and records of transactions.

claims.name: Unpaid rent by tenant
claims.example: Your tenant owes $3,000 in unpaid rent over the past three months and has vacated the property.
claims.notes: You have 3 years from the date rent was due to file. Bring the lease agreement, rent ledger, and any notices sent to the tenant.

claims.name: Payment with a bad check
claims.example: A customer paid for $1,500 worth of goods with a check that was returned for insufficient funds.
claims.notes: You have 3 years from the date the check was dishonored to file. Provide the original check, bank notice of dishonor, and any related correspondence.
claims.damage_boost: Check amount + service charge up to $35 + civil penalty up to $500 or 2x check amount, whichever is greater.

claims.name: Co-signer seeking repayment
claims.example: You co-signed a $4,000 loan for a friend who defaulted, and you had to pay the lender. Now you're seeking repayment from your friend.
claims.notes: You have 3 years from the date you made the payment to file. Bring the loan agreement, proof of your payment, and any communication with the borrower.

claim_categories.title: Consumer disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Maryland provides a 3-year statute of limitations for most consumer-related claims, including defective products and false advertising.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective product
claims.example: You bought a $1,200 refrigerator that stopped working after two months, and the seller refuses to repair or replace it.
claims.claim_statute: Md. Code Com. Law § 13-101 et seq.
claims.notes: You have 3 years from the date of purchase to file. Bring the purchase receipt, warranty information, and any communication with the seller.

claims.name: Substandard service provided
claims.example: You paid $800 for a home cleaning service that left your house in worse condition, and the company refuses to refund.
claims.claim_statute: Md. Code Com. Law § 13-101 et seq.
claims.notes: You have 3 years from the date of service to file. Provide the service agreement, payment proof, and photos of the substandard work.

claims.name: Faulty auto repair
claims.example: A mechanic charged you $2,500 for repairs that didn't fix the issue, and now refuses to address the problem.
claims.claim_statute: Md. Code Com. Law § 13-101 et seq.
claims.notes: You have 3 years from the date of repair to file. Bring the repair invoice, payment proof, and any subsequent repair assessments.

claims.name: Unsatisfactory work by a contractor
claims.example: You hired a contractor for $4,500 to renovate your bathroom, but the work was incomplete and subpar.
claims.claim_statute: Md. Code Com. Law § 13-101 et seq.
claims.notes: You have 3 years from the date of completion to file. Provide the contract, payment records, and photos of the work.

claims.name: Vendor failed to deliver services
claims.example: You paid a DJ $1,000 in advance for your wedding, but they never showed up and refuse to refund.
claims.claim_statute: Md. Code Com. Law § 13-101 et seq.
claims.notes: You have 3 years from the event date to file. Bring the contract, payment proof, and any communication with the vendor.

claims.name: Unauthorized charges after subscription cancellation
claims.example: You canceled a $50/month gym membership, but they continued to charge your card for three months.
claims.claim_statute: Md. Code Com. Law § 13-101 et seq.
claims.notes: You have 3 years from the first unauthorized charge to file. Provide cancellation confirmation and bank statements showing the charges.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective used car
claims.example: You bought a used car for $4,800 that broke down within a week, and the dealer refuses to address the issue.
claims.claim_statute: Md. Code Com. Law § 13-101 et seq.
claims.notes: You have 3 years from the purchase date to file. Bring the sales contract, payment proof, and any repair assessments.

claims.name: Misleading advertising leading to financial loss
claims.example: A store advertised a 'buy one, get one free' offer, but charged you full price for both items totaling $200.
claims.claim_statute: Md. Code Com. Law § 13-101 et seq.
claims.notes: You have 3 years from the date of purchase to file. Provide the advertisement, receipt, and any communication with the store.

claim_categories.title: Property damage
claim_categories.blurb: Maryland allows 3 years to file claims related to property damage, including vehicle collisions and damage to rental properties.

claims.name: Damage from a vehicle collision
claims.example: Another driver ran a red light and caused $4,500 in damages to your car.
claims.notes: You have 3 years from the date of the accident to file. Bring the police report, repair estimates, and photos of the damage.

claims.name: Neighbor's actions damaged your property
claims.example: Your neighbor's tree fell onto your fence, causing $2,000 in damages.
claims.notes: You have 3 years from the date of damage to file. Provide photos, repair estimates, and any communication with the neighbor.

claims.name: Tenant caused damage to rental property
claims.example: A tenant left your rental unit with $3,500 in damages beyond normal wear and tear.
claims.notes: You have 3 years from the date of discovery to file. Bring the lease agreement, photos of the damage, and repair invoices.

claims.name: Damage caused by someone else's pet
claims.example: A neighbor's dog entered your yard and destroyed $1,200 worth of landscaping.
claims.notes: You have 3 years from the date of damage to file. Provide photos, repair estimates, and any communication with the pet owner.

claims.name: Damage to property left in someone's care
claims.example: You left your laptop with a repair shop, and they returned it with a cracked screen.
claims.notes: You have 3 years from the date of damage to file. Bring the service agreement, photos of the damage, and repair estimates.

claim_categories.title: Landlord-tenant disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Maryland provides specific statutes for landlord-tenant disputes, including security deposits and uninhabitable rental conditions.

claims.name: Failure to return security deposit
claims.example: Your landlord withheld your $1,500 security deposit without providing an itemized list of damages.
claims.notes: You have 3 years from the end of tenancy to file. Bring the lease agreement, move-out inspection report, and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Security deposit deductions not itemized
claims.example: Your landlord deducted $800 from your deposit without providing an itemized list of damages.
claims.notes: You have 3 years from the end of tenancy to file. Provide the lease agreement, move-out inspection report, and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Rental unit deemed uninhabitable
claims.example: Your apartment had no heating during winter, and the landlord refused to make repairs.
claims.notes: You have 3 years from the date of issue to file. Bring photos, repair requests, and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Landlord wrongfully retained tenant's property
claims.example: After eviction, your landlord kept your furniture worth $2,000.
claims.notes: You have 3 years from the date of retention to file. Provide the lease agreement, inventory of items, and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Landlord conducted an illegal eviction
claims.example: Your landlord changed the locks without notice, preventing you from accessing your apartment.
claims.notes: You have 3 years from the date of eviction to file. Bring the lease agreement, photos of the changed locks, and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Failure to return prepaid rent after lease termination
claims.example: You paid first and last month's rent, but the landlord didn't return the last month's rent after you moved out early.
claims.notes: You have 3 years from the end of tenancy to file. Provide the lease agreement, proof of payment, and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Tenant failed to pay rent
claims.example: Your tenant owes $4,000 in unpaid rent over the past four months.
claims.notes: You have 3 years from the date rent was due to file. Bring the lease agreement, rent ledger, and any notices sent to the tenant.

claims.name: Tenant repaired and deducted from rent
claims.example: You paid $1,200 to fix a leaking roof after the landlord ignored repair requests and deducted it from your rent.
claims.notes: You have 3 years from the date of repair to file. Provide repair invoices, photos, and any communication with the landlord.

claim_categories.title: Employment and wages
claim_categories.blurb: Maryland allows 3 years to file claims related to unpaid wages, including final paychecks and overtime.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay wages
claims.example: Your employer owes you $4,500 in unpaid wages over the past two months.
claims.notes: You have 3 years from the date wages were due to file. Bring pay stubs, employment contract, and any communication with the employer.

claims.name: Employer withheld final paycheck
claims.example: After resigning, your employer refused to pay your final paycheck of $2,000.
claims.notes: You have 3 years from the date the final paycheck was due to file. Provide resignation letter, pay stubs, and any communication with the employer.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay earned commissions
claims.example: You earned $3,500 in sales commissions that your employer refuses to pay.
claims.notes: You have 3 years from the date commissions were due to file. Bring the commission agreement, sales records, and any communication with the employer.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay overtime wages
claims.example: You worked 20 hours of overtime last month, but your employer didn't pay the additional $1,000 owed.
claims.notes: You have 3 years from the date overtime was due to file. Provide timesheets, pay stubs, and any communication with the employer.

claim_categories.title: Personal claims
claim_categories.blurb: Maryland allows 3 years to file claims related to personal injuries and property disputes.

claims.name: Minor personal injury
claims.example: You slipped on an unmarked wet floor in a store, resulting in $4,000 in medical bills.
claims.notes: You have 3 years from the date of injury to file. Bring medical records, bills, and any incident reports.

claims.name: Damage to loaned property
claims.example: You lent a friend your camera worth $1,500, and they returned it broken.
claims.notes: You have 3 years from the date of damage to file. Provide proof of ownership, photos of the damage, and any communication with the friend.

claims.name: Theft of personal property
claims.example: A neighbor took your bicycle worth $800 without permission and refuses to return it.
claims.notes: You have 3 years from the date of theft to file. Bring proof of ownership, police report, and any communication with the neighbor.

claim_categories.title: Vehicle-specific claims
claim_categories.blurb: Maryland allows 3 years to file claims related to vehicle disputes, including mechanic issues and wrongful towing.

claims.name: Dispute over vehicle repairs
claims.example: A mechanic charged you $3,000 for repairs that didn't fix the issue, and now refuses to address the problem.
claims.claim_statute: Md. Code Com. Law § 13-101 et seq.
claims.notes: You have 3 years from the date of repair to file. Bring the repair invoice, payment proof, and any subsequent repair assessments.

claims.name: Vehicle was wrongfully towed
claims.example: Your car was towed from a legal parking spot, and you had to pay $500 to retrieve it.
claims.claim_statute: Md. Code Com. Law § 13-101 et seq.
claims.notes: You have 3 years from the date of towing to file. Provide towing receipts, photos of the parking spot, and any communication with the towing company.

exclusions.explanation: Eviction cases are handled separately from small claims and have specific procedures.
Disputes involving ownership of real estate are beyond the jurisdiction of small claims court.
Claims involving defamation require more formal procedures and are not suitable for small claims court.
Professional malpractice claims are complex and exceed the jurisdiction of small claims court.
Issues such as divorce, child custody, and support are handled by family courts.
Estate and will disputes are managed by probate courts.
Claims against the federal government are outside the jurisdiction of state courts.
Class action lawsuits are complex and not suitable for small claims court.
Small claims court cannot issue orders requiring someone to do or stop doing something.
Claims for workplace injuries are handled by the Workers' Compensation Commission.
Disputes subject to mandatory arbitration clauses are not handled in small claims court.
Claims against deceased individuals must be filed against their estate.

damages.punitive_explanation: Punitive damages are not awarded in small claims court; however, statutory penalties may apply in certain cases, such as bad check claims.

statutory_multipliers.explanation: Check amount + service charge up to $35 + civil penalty up to $500 or 2x check amount, whichever is greater.

damages.attorney_fees_explanation: Attorney fees may be recoverable if specified by statute or contract; otherwise, each party bears their own costs.

filing_options: Any county where ONE defendant resides
Where the cause of action arose

where_to_file.consequences_of_wrong_venue: Filing in the wrong venue may result in dismissal or transfer of the case, potentially causing delays and additional costs.
where_to_file.e_filing_notes: E-filing availability varies by county; check with the local District Court for specific information.

forms.name: Request for Waiver of Prepaid Costs

eligibility_bullets: Receiving need-based public assistance (SNAP, TANF, SSI)
Household income below 125% federal poverty line
waiver.coverage_notes: The waiver covers filing fees and service costs.
waiver.if_you_win_notes: If you win, the waived fees are added to the judgment against the loser.

fees.fees_recoverable_from_loser_notes: If you win, filing fees and reasonable service costs are added to the judgment.

entries.clock_starts: Date of last payment or charge

statute_of_limitations.discovery_rule_notes: The discovery rule applies to fraud claims, starting the clock when the fraud is discovered.
statute_of_limitations.tolling_notes: The statute of limitations may be tolled if the defendant is out of state, the plaintiff is a minor, or in cases of bankruptcy stays.

pre_filing.demand_letter_notes: While not required, sending a demand letter can often resolve disputes without court intervention.
pre_filing.bad_check_notice_rule: A 30-day written notice is required before seeking civil penalties for a bad check.
pre_filing.wage_claim_demand_rule: No specific demand requirement for wage claims, but written demand may support your case.
pre_filing.landlord_tenant_pre_filing_notes: Landlords must provide an itemized list of damages within 45 days of lease termination when withholding a security deposit.
pre_filing.consumer_protection_notes: No specific pre-filing requirements for consumer protection claims.

forms.name: Complaint
forms.description: Initiates a small claims case by outlining the plaintiff's allegations and desired relief.

forms.name: Affidavit of Service
forms.description: Proof that the defendant has been properly served with court documents.

forms.name: Request for Judgment
forms.description: Filed when seeking a default judgment due to defendant's failure to respond.

forms.name: Notice of Intention to Defend
forms.description: Filed by the defendant to indicate their intention to contest the claim.

forms.name: Counterclaim
forms.description: Filed by the defendant to assert their own claim against the plaintiff.

forms.name: Request for Waiver of Prepaid Costs
forms.description: Request to waive filing and service fees due to financial hardship.

forms.name: Notice of Appeal
forms.description: Filed to appeal the District Court's decision to the Circuit Court.

methods.name: Sheriff personal service
methods.description: Sheriff or constable personally hands the papers to the defendant.

methods.name: Certified mail
methods.description: Court clerk sends documents via certified mail to the defendant.

methods.name: Private process server
methods.description: A private individual or company delivers the documents to the defendant.

service.business_service_rule: Serve the registered agent or an officer of the corporation at the business's principal office.
service.out_of_state_defendant_notes: Service must comply with Maryland rules and the laws of the state where the defendant is located.
service.cant_find_defendant_notes: If the defendant cannot be located, you may request alternative service methods, such as posting or publication, with court approval.
service.avoiding_service_notes: If the defendant is evading service, document your attempts and request alternative service methods from the court.

response_and_default.response_notes: Defendants are encouraged to file a Notice of Intention to Defend within 15 days of service to contest the claim.
response_and_default.default_process: If the defendant does not respond or appear, the court may enter a default judgment in favor of the plaintiff.
response_and_default.prove_up_notes: Even in default cases, the plaintiff must provide evidence to support the claim before a judgment is entered.
response_and_default.motion_to_vacate_appeal_notes: If the motion to vacate is denied, you may appeal the decision within 30 days.

counterclaim.transfer_to_higher_court_notes: If the counterclaim exceeds $5,000, the case may be transferred to the Circuit Court.

hearing.lawyers_allowed_notes: Parties may choose to be represented by an attorney, but it is not required.
hearing.format: Bench trial with informal procedures; each side presents their case directly to the judge.
what_to_bring: Names and contact info for witnesses
hearing.interpreter_request_process: Request an interpreter at least 30 days before the hearing by contacting the court clerk.
hearing.decision_timing: On the spot or mailed within 30 days
hearing.on_site_mediation_notes: Some counties offer mediation services on-site; check with the local court for availability.

appeals.type_notes: The case is retried in the Circuit Court as if the original trial had not occurred.
appeals.default_judgment_notes: A default judgment can be appealed within 30 days; however, filing a motion to vacate may be a more efficient first step.

collection.post_judgment_interest_notes: Interest accrues on the unpaid judgment amount until fully paid.

methods.name: Wage garnishment
methods.blurb: Have a portion of the debtor's wages withheld until the judgment is paid.
methods.description: File a Request for Writ of Garnishment of Wages (DC-CV-065) with the court. Once issued, serve the writ on the debtor's employer, who will withhold a portion of the debtor's wages and remit them to the court until the judgment is satisfied.

forms.name: Request for Writ of Garnishment of Wages

methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor is employed and earns above the exemption threshold.
exemptions: 75% of disposable earnings per week or 30 times the federal minimum hourly wage, whichever is greater.

methods.name: Bank account garnishment
methods.blurb: Seize funds from the debtor's bank account to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: File a Request for Writ of Garnishment of Property (DC-CV-060) with the court. Once issued, serve the writ on the debtor's bank, which will freeze the account up to the amount of the judgment. The bank will then remit the funds to the court.

forms.name: Request for Writ of Garnishment of Property

methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor has sufficient funds in the account; joint accounts may present challenges.
exemptions: $6,000 in a bank account is exempt from garnishment.

methods.name: Lien on real property
methods.blurb: Place a lien on the debtor's real property to secure the judgment debt.
methods.description: File a Notice of Lien (DC-CV-035) with the court. Once recorded, the lien attaches to the debtor's real property, potentially preventing its sale or refinancing until the judgment is satisfied.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective in securing the debt, but may not result in immediate payment unless the property is sold or refinanced.

methods.name: Writ of execution
methods.blurb: Seize and sell the debtor's non-exempt personal property to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: File a Request for Writ of Execution (DC-CV-040) with the court. Once issued, the sheriff will seize the debtor's non-exempt property and sell it at public auction, applying the proceeds to the judgment.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor owns valuable non-exempt property; process can be time-consuming.
exemptions: Personal property up to $5,000 is exempt from execution.

collection.judgment_renewal_process: File a motion to renew the judgment before the 12-year period expires; no additional fee required.

debtor_exam_form.name: Request for Order Directing Defendant to Appear for Examination in Aid of Enforcement of Judgment

collection.bankruptcy_effect_notes: Filing for bankruptcy imposes an automatic stay on collection efforts; certain judgments may be discharged in bankruptcy.
collection.priority_notes: Priority among multiple judgment creditors is generally determined by the order in which liens are recorded.

businesses.self_representation_notes: Businesses may represent themselves in small claims court without an attorney.
businesses.fictitious_name_notes: Businesses must use their legal entity name when filing or defending a case; trade names alone are insufficient.
businesses.sole_proprietor_employee_exception: Sole proprietors may represent themselves or be represented by an employee with proper authorization.
businesses.licensing_notes: Unlicensed contractors may face challenges in enforcing contracts; consult legal counsel for specific situations.

pitfalls.title: Incorrect defendant information
pitfalls.what_happens: Suing the wrong party or using incorrect names can result in dismissal or unenforceable judgments.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Verify the exact legal name and address of the defendant before filing; for businesses, check the State Department of Assessments and Taxation records.

pitfalls.title: Missing statute of limitations
pitfalls.what_happens: Filing after the statute of limitations has expired will lead to dismissal of your case.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Confirm the applicable statute of limitations for your claim type and file within the allowed time frame.

pitfalls.title: Improper service of process
pitfalls.what_happens: Failure to properly serve the defendant can delay your case or result in dismissal.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Follow Maryland's rules for service of process carefully, using authorized methods and documenting service properly.

pitfalls.title: Insufficient evidence
pitfalls.what_happens: Lack of evidence can lead to losing your case, even if your claim is valid.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Gather all relevant documents, photos, and witness statements before filing and bring them to court.

pitfalls.title: Ignoring counterclaims
pitfalls.what_happens: If the defendant files a counterclaim and you don't respond, you could lose by default.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Respond promptly to any counterclaims and prepare your defense accordingly.

pitfalls.title: Overlooking collection challenges
pitfalls.what_happens: Winning a judgment doesn't guarantee payment; collection can be difficult.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Before filing, consider the defendant's ability to pay and be prepared to take additional steps to collect if necessary.

faqs.question: Do I need a lawyer?
faqs.answer: No, you are not required to have a lawyer in small claims court. The procedures are simplified to allow individuals to represent themselves effectively.

faqs.question: How long does it take?
faqs.answer: Small claims cases are typically scheduled for trial within 60 days of filing, but the exact timeline can vary depending on the court's schedule.

faqs.question: What's the maximum I can sue for?
faqs.answer: In Maryland's small claims court, you can sue for up to $5,000, excluding interest, costs, and attorney's fees.

faqs.question: What happens if the defendant doesn't show up?
faqs.answer: If the defendant fails to appear, the court may enter a default judgment in your favor, provided you present sufficient evidence to support your claim.

faqs.question: Can I appeal?
faqs.answer: Yes, either party can appeal a small claims judgment to the Circuit Court within 30 days. The appeal is heard as a new trial (trial de novo).

faqs.question: How long is a judgment good for?
faqs.answer: A judgment in Maryland is valid for 12 years and can be renewed for another 12 years if necessary.

state_specific_notes: Maryland's small claims court has a jurisdictional limit of $5,000, excluding interest, costs, and attorney's fees.
The District Court has exclusive jurisdiction over small claims cases, meaning they cannot be filed in Circuit Court.
Small claims procedures are designed to be informal, allowing individuals to represent themselves without an attorney.
Discovery procedures, such as interrogatories and depositions, are not permitted in small claims cases.
Appeals from small claims judgments are heard de novo in the Circuit Court, meaning the case is retried as if the original trial had not occurred.

interesting_findings: Maryland's small claims court emphasizes informal procedures to facilitate self-representation.
The state provides specific statutory penalties for bad check claims, including potential civil penalties up to $500.
Maryland allows for the recovery of attorney's fees in certain cases, particularly when specified by statute or contract.
The state's small claims court does not permit pre-trial discovery, streamlining the litigation process.
Maryland's post-judgment interest rate is set at 10%, providing a significant incentive for timely payment of judgments.

sources.label: Md. Code Com. Law § 13-101 et seq.
sources.citation: Md. Code Com. Law § 13-101 et seq.
